description:
This explanatory diagram illustrates common geometric distortions in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery caused by terrain relief, specifically foreshortening, lay-over, and shadow. The diagram shows a cross-section of a mountain profile labelled from A to A' along a horizontal "Ground Range" axis. A diagonal line represents the "Slant Range" radar signal path. As the radar beam illuminates the terrain:
1.  **Foreshortening** occurs when the radar signal interacts with the front slope of the mountain, compressing a longer ground distance into a shorter slant range distance, depicted by a light grey block in the slant range representation.
2.  **Lay-over** occurs when the top of a feature is recorded before its base. This is shown where signals from the mountain peak overlap signals from its lower parts, resulting in white blocks in the slant range representation. This phenomenon is depicted for both mountain peaks.
3.  **Shadow** occurs on the far side of the mountain, where the terrain blocks the radar signal, resulting in no return. This is represented by a black block in the slant range.
An inset grayscale SAR image, approximately 380x370 pixels, depicts a volcanic-like mountain, with axes labelled "azimuth" (horizontal) and "range" (vertical). A white vertical line on the SAR image, labelled A-A', corresponds to the cross-section shown in the terrain profile, illustrating how these distortions appear in real SAR data.
